[05/04/25, 11:39:31 PM] Shreya (ai): Got it — no more baby questions! Let’s turn this up a notch with:


---

1. Legal Latin Terms You Should Know (with Examples)

These are often used in courtroom dramatizations, judgments, and legal arguments.


---

2. Advanced Legal Scenarios for Analysis & Creativity

Scenario 1: Entrapment or Not?
An undercover cop persuades a man to sell drugs. The man initially refuses but eventually agrees. Is this entrapment or valid law enforcement?
Think in terms of intent, opportunity, and coercion.

Scenario 2: Crime Scene vs Alibi Conflict
The accused claims to have been at a wedding during a murder. However, a time-stamped metro card shows he left the area just 20 minutes before the crime. How do you argue for or against the alibi in court?

Scenario 3: Chain of Custody Broken
A knife was found at the crime scene, but the officer forgot to sign the evidence log. The defense argues it could have been tampered with.
Is the evidence admissible? Why or why not?


---

3. Reading/Watching Material (Intermediate–Advanced)

Books & PDFs

"Law 101" by Jay Feinman – Explains core legal principles in real-world terms.

"Criminal Law: Cases and Materials" – Get into actual judgments, case laws, and reasoning.

Bare Acts – Read Indian Penal Code (IPC), CrPC, and Evidence Act sections. Start with IPC Section 299–304 for homicide concepts.


Web Resources

Indian Kanoon – Browse actual judgments, petitions, and legal texts.

LiveLaw – Stay updated with recent court decisions and analysis.

Bar & Bench – Excellent for courtroom commentary and legal journalism.


TV/Movies (Courtroom Drama with Depth)

The Trial (Netflix India) – Based on real court dynamics.

Suits – US-based but heavy on strategy and legal maneuvering.

12 Angry Men – Focuses entirely on jury dynamics and critical reasoning.

Boston Legal / The Practice – Older, but brilliant on courtroom tactics.



---

[29/04/25, 12:46:59 PM] Naina Jain: #include <iostream>
#include <list>
using namespace std;

class PriorityQueue {
private:
    list<pair<int, int>> q; // {priority, value}

public:
    void enqueue(int value, int priority) {
        auto it = q.begin();
        while (it != q.end() && it->first <= priority) {
            ++it;
        }
        q.insert(it, {priority, value});  // Insert before higher priority
    }

    void dequeue() {
        if (q.empty()) {
            cout << "Queue is empty.\n";
            return;
        }
        q.pop_front();
    }

    int peek() {
        if (q.empty()) {
            cout << "Queue is empty.\n";
            return -1;
        }
        return q.front().second;
    }

    bool isEmpty() {
        return q.empty();
    }
};

int main() {
    PriorityQueue pq;

    int n;
    cout << "Enter number of elements: ";
    cin >> n;

    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        int value, priority;
        cout << "Enter value and its priority: ";
        cin >> value >> priority;
        pq.enqueue(value, priority);
    }

    cout << "\nServing elements by priority:\n";
    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
        cout << "Next: " << pq.peek() << endl;
        pq.dequeue();
    }

    return 0;
}

(These rights cannot be suspended, even during an emergency)
•	Articles 23–24
Right against Exploitation (prohibition of trafficking, forced labor, and child labor)
•	Articles 25–28
Right to Freedom of Religion
•	Articles 29–30
Cultural and Educational Rights
•	Articles 32–35
Right to Constitutional Remedies (enforce Fundamental Rights via courts)
•	Article 43-B
Directive Principle for promoting cooperative societies (added by 97th Amendment)
•	Articles 243-ZH to 243-ZT
Provisions related to Co-operative Societies (Part IX-B)
•	Article 352
Emergency due to war, external aggression, or armed rebellion
•	Articles 356 & 365
Emergency due to failure of constitutional machinery in states (President’s Rule)
•	Article 360
Financial Emergency
________________________________________
🛠️ Constitutional Amendments Mentioned
•	42nd Constitutional Amendment Act (1976)
o	Added the words "Socialist" and "Secular" to the Preamble
o	Introduced Fundamental Duties under Article 51-A
o	Enhanced Directive Principles' importance
•	73rd Constitutional Amendment Act (1992)
o	Granted constitutional status to Panchayati Raj (Part IX, Schedule 11)
•	74th Constitutional Amendment Act (1992)
o	Recognized Municipalities as Urban Local Bodies (Part IX-A, Schedule 12)
•	86th Constitutional Amendment Act (2002)
o	Added the 11th Fundamental Duty:
“Provide education to children aged 6 to 14 years”
•	97th Constitutional Amendment Act (2011)
o	Gave constitutional recognition to Co-operative Societies
o	Made the right to form co-operative societies a Fundamental Right (Article 19)
o	Added Article 43-B to the Directive Principles
o	Inserted Part IX-B (Articles 243-ZH to 243-ZT)

[08/09/25, 10:38:43 AM] Shreya (ai): Got it 👍 Let’s carefully start from 900, the very first arrival, and walk step by step.

We have:

Arrivals: [900, 940, 950, 1100, 1500, 1800]

Departures: [910, 1120, 1130, 1200, 1900, 2000] (sorted already)



---

Step-by-step simulation

🔹 At 900 → First train arrives.
platforms_needed = 1, max_platforms = 1.


---

🔹 Next compare arrival[1] = 940 with departure[0] = 910:

Since 910 < 940, the 900-train departs before the 940-train arrives.

So, platforms_needed = 1 - 1 = 0, move departure pointer → j=1.


Now compare again: arrival[1] = 940 vs departure[1] = 1120:

940 < 1120, so a new train arrives before departure.

platforms_needed = 0 + 1 = 1, move arrival pointer → i=2.

max_platforms = max(1,1) = 1.



---

🔹 Compare arrival[2] = 950 with departure[1] = 1120:

950 < 1120, another train arrives.

platforms_needed = 1 + 1 = 2, move arrival pointer → i=3.

max_platforms = max(2,1) = 2.



---

🔹 Compare arrival[3] = 1100 with departure[1] = 1120:

1100 < 1120, another train arrives.

platforms_needed = 2 + 1 = 3, move arrival pointer → i=4.

max_platforms = max(3,2) = 3.



---

🔹 Compare arrival[4] = 1500 with departure[1] = 1120:

1120 < 1500, so a departure happens.

platforms_needed = 3 - 1 = 2, move departure pointer → j=2.


Now departure[2] = 1130 < 1500:

Another departure → platforms_needed = 2 - 1 = 1, j=3.


Now departure[3] = 1200 < 1500:

Another departure → platforms_needed = 1 - 1 = 0, j=4.


Now compare arrival[4] = 1500 with departure[4] = 1900:

1500 < 1900, so train arrives.

platforms_needed = 0 + 1 = 1, i=5.



---

🔹 Compare arrival[5] = 1800 with departure[4] = 1900:

1800 < 1900, so train arrives.

platforms_needed = 1 + 1 = 2, i=6 (done with arrivals).



---

✅ Final Answer

The maximum platforms_needed at any point = 3.
So, the railway station needs minimum 3 platforms.


---

Do you want me to also draw a timeline (like a chart of trains occupying platforms) so it’s even more visual?
‎[08/09/25, 11:07:44 AM] Manav: ‎image omitted
‎[08/09/25, 11:07:55 AM] Manav: ‎image omitted

‎[08/09/25, 11:22:39 AM] Neel Lapsiwala: Signal and Systems - Lesson Plan 2025-26.pdf • ‎1 page ‎document omitted
[08/09/25, 12:45:58 PM] Naina Jain: Algorithm (Two-Array Sweep Method)

Input: list of intervals [start, end)
Output: minimum number of classrooms required

1. Put all start times into array S.


2. Put all end times into array E.


3. Sort S and E independently.


4. Initialize:

i = 0 (index for starts),

j = 0 (index for ends),

rooms_in_use = 0,

max_rooms = 0.



5. While i < n (still have lectures to process):

If S[i] < E[j] → a lecture starts before the next one ends:

rooms_in_use++

max_rooms = max(max_rooms, rooms_in_use)

i++


Else (S[i] >= E[j]) → some lecture has ended:

rooms_in_use--

j++




6. At the end, max_rooms is the answer.




---

Calculation on Example

Lectures:

L1: [09:00, 10:30)

L2: [09:15, 10:00)

L3: [10:00, 11:30)

L4: [10:15, 11:00)

L5: [11:00, 12:30)

L6: [11:15, 12:00)


Step 1. Arrays:

Starts S = [09:00, 09:15, 10:00, 10:15, 11:00, 11:15]

Ends   E = [10:30, 10:00, 11:30, 11:00, 12:30, 12:00]


Step 2. Sort both:

S = [09:00, 09:15, 10:00, 10:15, 11:00, 11:15]

E = [10:00, 10:30, 11:00, 11:30, 12:00, 12:30]


Step 3. Walk through:

09:00 (S[0] < E[0]) → start → rooms=1, max=1

09:15 (S[1] < E[0]) → start → rooms=2, max=2

10:00 (S[2] == E[0]) → end first → rooms=1, then start → rooms=2

10:15 (S[3] < E[1]=10:30) → start → rooms=3, max=3

10:30 (S[4]=11:00 ≥ E[1]) → end → rooms=2

11:00 (S[4]==E[2]) → end → rooms=1, then start → rooms=2

11:15 (S[5] < E[3]=11:30) → start → rooms=3, max still=3

Remaining ends reduce count gradually.


Step 4. Result:
Maximum overlap seen = 3

[17/09/25, 6:46:08 PM] Shreya (ai): Awesome — here are 7 practice problems with full solutions in all 4 ISA styles (Stack, Accumulator, Memory-memory, Register). Each problem is shown first, then the four ISA solutions right after it so you can test yourself and then check the answer. I’ll keep the instruction sequences short and realistic (typical pseudo-assembly you’ll see in exams).

I’ll use the same notation as your example:

Stack: PUSH, POP, arithmetic ops that pop top two, push result.

Accumulator: single ACC register; LOAD X puts X in ACC; ops operate on ACC; STORE T stores ACC to memory.

Memory-memory: direct memory operations (op dest, src) where possible — e.g. ADD A, B means A = A + B. If a temporary result is needed, I’ll store to a memory temp T1, T2.

Register (GPR): LOAD R#, <mem> then OP Rd, Rs, Rt style and STORE <mem>, R#.



---

1) Problem: (A + B) * (C - D)

Stack:

PUSH A
PUSH B
ADD         ; stack: (A+B)
PUSH C
PUSH D
SUB         ; stack: (C-D)
MUL         ; (A+B)*(C-D) on top

Accumulator:

LOAD A       ; ACC = A
ADD B        ; ACC = A + B
STORE T1     ; T1 = A+B

LOAD C       ; ACC = C
SUB D        ; ACC = C - D
STORE T2     ; T2 = C-D

LOAD T1      ; ACC = A+B
MUL T2       ; ACC = (A+B)*(C-D)

Memory-Memory:

ADD T1, A,B    ; T1 = A + B   (pseudo: ADD dest, src1,src2 collapsed)
SUB T2, C,D    ; T2 = C - D
MUL T3, T1,T2  ; T3 = T1 * T2

(If architecture only supports two-operand memory ops: ADD T1, A then ADD T1, B etc.)

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, A
LOAD R2, B
ADD  R3, R1, R2    ; R3 = A + B

LOAD R4, C
LOAD R5, D
SUB  R6, R4, R5    ; R6 = C - D

MUL  R7, R3, R6    ; R7 = (A+B)*(C-D)
STORE RESULT, R7


---

2) Problem: A / (B + C) + D

Stack:

PUSH B
PUSH C
ADD         ; (B+C)
PUSH A
SWAP? or better sequence:
PUSH A
PUSH B
PUSH C
ADD         ; stack: A, (B+C)
DIV         ; A/(B+C)
PUSH D
ADD         ; A/(B+C) + D

(If stack machine has SWAP, use it; here we push A then the sum then DIV assumes top two: dividend, divisor.)

Accumulator:

LOAD B
ADD C         ; ACC = B + C
STORE T1

LOAD A
DIV T1        ; ACC = A / (B + C)
ADD D         ; ACC = A/(B+C) + D

Memory-Memory:

ADD T1, B,C    ; T1 = B + C
DIV T2, A,T1   ; T2 = A / T1
ADD T3, T2,D   ; T3 = A/(B+C) + D

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, B
LOAD R2, C
ADD  R3, R1, R2    ; R3 = B + C

LOAD R4, A
DIV  R5, R4, R3    ; R5 = A / (B+C)

LOAD R6, D
ADD  R7, R5, R6    ; R7 = A/(B+C) + D
STORE RESULT, R7


---

3) Problem: A*B + C/D

Stack:

PUSH A
PUSH B
MUL         ; (A*B)
PUSH C
PUSH D
DIV         ; (C/D)
ADD         ; (A*B) + (C/D)

Accumulator:

LOAD A
MUL B        ; ACC = A * B
STORE T1

LOAD C
DIV D        ; ACC = C / D
STORE T2

LOAD T1
ADD T2       ; ACC = A*B + C/D

Memory-Memory:

MUL T1, A,B   ; T1 = A*B
DIV T2, C,D   ; T2 = C/D
ADD T3, T1,T2 ; T3 = T1 + T2

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, A
LOAD R2, B
MUL  R3, R1, R2   ; R3 = A*B

LOAD R4, C
LOAD R5, D
DIV  R6, R4, R5   ; R6 = C/D

ADD  R7, R3, R6   ; R7 = A*B + C/D
STORE RESULT, R7


---

4) Problem: (A - B) * (C + D) / E  (i.e. ((A-B)*(C+D))/E)

Stack:

PUSH A
PUSH B
SUB         ; (A-B)
PUSH C
PUSH D
ADD         ; (C+D)
MUL         ; (A-B)*(C+D)
PUSH E
DIV         ; result = ((A-B)*(C+D))/E

Accumulator:

LOAD A
SUB B        ; ACC = A - B
STORE T1

LOAD C
ADD D        ; ACC = C + D
STORE T2

LOAD T1
MUL T2       ; ACC = (A-B)*(C+D)
DIV E        ; ACC = ((A-B)*(C+D))/E

Memory-Memory:

SUB T1, A,B    ; T1 = A-B
ADD T2, C,D    ; T2 = C+D
MUL T3, T1,T2  ; T3 = (A-B)*(C+D)
DIV T4, T3,E   ; T4 = T3 / E

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, A
LOAD R2, B
SUB  R3, R1, R2   ; R3 = A-B

LOAD R4, C
LOAD R5, D
ADD  R6, R4, R5   ; R6 = C+D

MUL  R7, R3, R6   ; R7 = (A-B)*(C+D)
LOAD R8, E
DIV  R9, R7, R8   ; R9 = ((A-B)*(C+D))/E
STORE RESULT, R9


---

5) Problem: (A + B + C) / D

Stack:

PUSH A
PUSH B
ADD         ; A+B
PUSH C
ADD         ; (A+B)+C
PUSH D
DIV         ; (A+B+C)/D

Accumulator:

LOAD A
ADD B         ; ACC = A+B
ADD C         ; ACC = A+B+C
DIV D         ; ACC = (A+B+C)/D

Memory-Memory:

ADD T1, A,B    ; T1 = A + B
ADD T2, T1,C   ; T2 = A + B + C
DIV T3, T2,D   ; T3 = (A+B+C)/D

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, A
LOAD R2, B
ADD  R3, R1, R2   ; R3 = A+B

LOAD R4, C
ADD  R5, R3, R4   ; R5 = A+B+C

LOAD R6, D
DIV  R7, R5, R6   ; R7 = (A+B+C)/D
STORE RESULT, R7


---

6) Problem: A - (B * (C + D))

Stack:

PUSH B
PUSH C
PUSH D
ADD         ; (C+D)
MUL         ; B*(C+D)
PUSH A
SWAP or reorder: better push A then computed product:
PUSH A
PUSH B
PUSH C
PUSH D
ADD
MUL         ; stack: A, (B*(C+D))
SUB         ; A - (B*(C+D))

Accumulator:

LOAD C
ADD D        ; ACC = C + D
MUL B        ; ACC = B*(C+D)
STORE T1

LOAD A
SUB T1       ; ACC = A - (B*(C+D))

Memory
[17/09/25, 6:47:04 PM] Shreya (ai): Awesome — here are 7 practice problems with full solutions in all 4 ISA styles (Stack, Accumulator, Memory-memory, Register). Each problem is shown first, then the four ISA solutions right after it so you can test yourself and then check the answer. I’ll keep the instruction sequences short and realistic (typical pseudo-assembly you’ll see in exams).

I’ll use the same notation as your example:

Stack: PUSH, POP, arithmetic ops that pop top two, push result.

Accumulator: single ACC register; LOAD X puts X in ACC; ops operate on ACC; STORE T stores ACC to memory.

Memory-memory: direct memory operations (op dest, src) where possible — e.g. ADD A, B means A = A + B. If a temporary result is needed, I’ll store to a memory temp T1, T2.

Register (GPR): LOAD R#, <mem> then OP Rd, Rs, Rt style and STORE <mem>, R#.



---

1) Problem: (A + B) * (C - D)

Stack:

PUSH A
PUSH B
ADD         ; stack: (A+B)
PUSH C
PUSH D
SUB         ; stack: (C-D)
MUL         ; (A+B)*(C-D) on top

Accumulator:

LOAD A       ; ACC = A
ADD B        ; ACC = A + B
STORE T1     ; T1 = A+B

LOAD C       ; ACC = C
SUB D        ; ACC = C - D
STORE T2     ; T2 = C-D

LOAD T1      ; ACC = A+B
MUL T2       ; ACC = (A+B)*(C-D)

Memory-Memory:

ADD T1, A,B    ; T1 = A + B   (pseudo: ADD dest, src1,src2 collapsed)
SUB T2, C,D    ; T2 = C - D
MUL T3, T1,T2  ; T3 = T1 * T2

(If architecture only supports two-operand memory ops: ADD T1, A then ADD T1, B etc.)

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, A
LOAD R2, B
ADD  R3, R1, R2    ; R3 = A + B

LOAD R4, C
LOAD R5, D
SUB  R6, R4, R5    ; R6 = C - D

MUL  R7, R3, R6    ; R7 = (A+B)*(C-D)
STORE RESULT, R7


---

2) Problem: A / (B + C) + D

Stack:

PUSH B
PUSH C
ADD         ; (B+C)
PUSH A
SWAP? or better sequence:
PUSH A
PUSH B
PUSH C
ADD         ; stack: A, (B+C)
DIV         ; A/(B+C)
PUSH D
ADD         ; A/(B+C) + D

(If stack machine has SWAP, use it; here we push A then the sum then DIV assumes top two: dividend, divisor.)

Accumulator:

LOAD B
ADD C         ; ACC = B + C
STORE T1

LOAD A
DIV T1        ; ACC = A / (B + C)
ADD D         ; ACC = A/(B+C) + D

Memory-Memory:

ADD T1, B,C    ; T1 = B + C
DIV T2, A,T1   ; T2 = A / T1
ADD T3, T2,D   ; T3 = A/(B+C) + D

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, B
LOAD R2, C
ADD  R3, R1, R2    ; R3 = B + C

LOAD R4, A
DIV  R5, R4, R3    ; R5 = A / (B+C)

LOAD R6, D
ADD  R7, R5, R6    ; R7 = A/(B+C) + D
STORE RESULT, R7


---

3) Problem: A*B + C/D

Stack:

PUSH A
PUSH B
MUL         ; (A*B)
PUSH C
PUSH D
DIV         ; (C/D)
ADD         ; (A*B) + (C/D)

Accumulator:

LOAD A
MUL B        ; ACC = A * B
STORE T1

LOAD C
DIV D        ; ACC = C / D
STORE T2

LOAD T1
ADD T2       ; ACC = A*B + C/D

Memory-Memory:

MUL T1, A,B   ; T1 = A*B
DIV T2, C,D   ; T2 = C/D
ADD T3, T1,T2 ; T3 = T1 + T2

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, A
LOAD R2, B
MUL  R3, R1, R2   ; R3 = A*B

LOAD R4, C
LOAD R5, D
DIV  R6, R4, R5   ; R6 = C/D

ADD  R7, R3, R6   ; R7 = A*B + C/D
STORE RESULT, R7


---

4) Problem: (A - B) * (C + D) / E  (i.e. ((A-B)*(C+D))/E)

Stack:

PUSH A
PUSH B
SUB         ; (A-B)
PUSH C
PUSH D
ADD         ; (C+D)
MUL         ; (A-B)*(C+D)
PUSH E
DIV         ; result = ((A-B)*(C+D))/E

Accumulator:

LOAD A
SUB B        ; ACC = A - B
STORE T1

LOAD C
ADD D        ; ACC = C + D
STORE T2

LOAD T1
MUL T2       ; ACC = (A-B)*(C+D)
DIV E        ; ACC = ((A-B)*(C+D))/E

Memory-Memory:

SUB T1, A,B    ; T1 = A-B
ADD T2, C,D    ; T2 = C+D
MUL T3, T1,T2  ; T3 = (A-B)*(C+D)
DIV T4, T3,E   ; T4 = T3 / E

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, A
LOAD R2, B
SUB  R3, R1, R2   ; R3 = A-B

LOAD R4, C
LOAD R5, D
ADD  R6, R4, R5   ; R6 = C+D

MUL  R7, R3, R6   ; R7 = (A-B)*(C+D)
LOAD R8, E
DIV  R9, R7, R8   ; R9 = ((A-B)*(C+D))/E
STORE RESULT, R9


---

5) Problem: (A + B + C) / D

Stack:

PUSH A
PUSH B
ADD         ; A+B
PUSH C
ADD         ; (A+B)+C
PUSH D
DIV         ; (A+B+C)/D

Accumulator:

LOAD A
ADD B         ; ACC = A+B
ADD C         ; ACC = A+B+C
DIV D         ; ACC = (A+B+C)/D

Memory-Memory:

ADD T1, A,B    ; T1 = A + B
ADD T2, T1,C   ; T2 = A + B + C
DIV T3, T2,D   ; T3 = (A+B+C)/D

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, A
LOAD R2, B
ADD  R3, R1, R2   ; R3 = A+B

LOAD R4, C
ADD  R5, R3, R4   ; R5 = A+B+C

LOAD R6, D
DIV  R7, R5, R6   ; R7 = (A+B+C)/D
STORE RESULT, R7


---

6) Problem: A - (B * (C + D))

Stack:

PUSH B
PUSH C
PUSH D
ADD         ; (C+D)
MUL         ; B*(C+D)
PUSH A
SWAP or reorder: better push A then computed product:
PUSH A
PUSH B
PUSH C
PUSH D
ADD
MUL         ; stack: A, (B*(C+D))
SUB         ; A - (B*(C+D))

Accumulator:

LOAD C
ADD D        ; ACC = C + D
MUL B        ; ACC = B*(C+D)
STORE T1

LOAD A
SUB T1       ; ACC = A - (B*(C+D))

Memory-Memory:

ADD T1, C,D    ; T1 = C + D
MUL T2, B,T1   ; T2 = B*(C+D)
SUB T3, A,T2   ; T3 = A - T2

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, C
LOAD R2, D
ADD  R3, R1, R2   ; R3 = C + D

LOAD R4, B
MUL  R5, R4, R3   ; R5 = B*(C+D)

LOAD R6, A
SUB  R7, R6, R5   ; R7 = A - (B*(C+D))
STORE RESULT, R7


---

7) Problem: (A * B) - (C / D) + E

Stack:

PUSH A
PUSH B
MUL         ; (A*B)
PUSH C
PUSH D
DIV         ; (C/D)
SUB         ; (A*B) - (C/D)
PUSH E
ADD         ; ((A*B) - (C/D)) + E

Accumulator:

LOAD A
MUL B        ; ACC = A*B
STORE T1

LOAD C
DIV D        ; ACC = C/D
STORE T2

LOAD T1
SUB T2       ; ACC = (A*B) - (C/D)
ADD E        ; ACC = (A*B) - (C/D) + E

Memory-Memory:

MUL T1, A,B   ; T1 = A*B
DIV T2, C,D   ; T2 = C/D
SUB T3, T1,T2 ; T3 = T1 - T2
ADD T4, T3,E  ; T4 = T3 + E

Register (GPR):

LOAD R1, A
LOAD R2, B
MUL  R3,

[18/09/25, 11:40:39 AM] Naina Jain: Perfect 👍 Now that we have a full set of micro-operations, I’ll give you MCQs (exam style) based on them. These will test both concepts and sequence understanding.


---

📘 MCQs on Micro-Operations & Control Sequences


---

Q1.

For the instruction INC R1, which of the following is the correct sequence?

A) R1out, ADD 1, Zin → Zout, R1in
B) R1out, Select4, SUB, Zin → Zout, R1in
C) R1out, Select4, ADD, Zin → Zout, R1in
D) R1out, Yin → R2out, ADD, Zin → Zout, R1in

Answer: C ✅


---

Q2.

In the instruction BZ label (Branch if Zero), the PC is updated only if:

A) Carry flag = 1
B) Z flag = 0
C) Z flag = 1
D) Negative flag = 1

Answer: C ✅


---

Q3.

For the instruction NOT R1, the ALU performs:

A) One’s complement on R1
B) Two’s complement on R1
C) Logical AND with 1
D) Shift left operation

Answer: A ✅


---

Q4.

Which of the following correctly represents the micro-operations for LOAD R1, X?

A) R1out → MDRin → MAR ← X → Read → MDRout → R1in
B) MAR ← X → Read → MDRin → MDRout → R1in
C) PCout → MARin → Read → MDRin → MDRout → R1in
D) IRout → R1in

Answer: B ✅


---

Q5.

For ADD R1, R2, which temporary register is used to hold the first operand?

A) Z
B) Y
C) MDR
D) PC

Answer: B ✅


---

Q6.

What is the first micro-operation in executing PUSH R1?

A) SPout, MARin
B) R1out, MDRin
C) SPout, Yin
D) SPout, ADD, Zin

Answer: C ✅


---

Q7.

The difference between BRANCH label and CALL label is:

A) BRANCH updates PC, CALL does not.
B) BRANCH saves PC on stack, CALL does not.
C) CALL saves PC on stack, BRANCH does not.
D) Both save PC on stack.

Answer: C ✅


---

Q8.

For the instruction CMP R1, R2, the final output is stored in:

A) Register R1
B) Register R2
C) Flag Register
D) Temporary register Y

Answer: C ✅


---

Q9.

Which instruction requires no micro-operations except End?

A) NOP
B) CLR
C) NEG
D) HLT

Answer: A ✅


---

Q10.

For SHR R1, which of the following is correct?

A) R1out, SHL, Zin → Zout, R1in
B) R1out, SHR, Zin → Zout, R1in
C) R1out, ROL, Zin → Zout, R1in
D) R1out, NOT, Zin → Zout, R1in

Answer: B ✅


---

[18/09/25, 3:41:29 PM] Shreya (ai): Nice — let’s smash these convos like scaling an Insta store to a website. Below are clear, step-by-step solutions for Problem 4 (continuous) and Problem 5 (discrete).

4 — Continuous convolution

Given
 for  (zero elsewhere)
 for  (zero elsewhere)
We want .

Because  is nonzero only for , the integral reduces to

y(t)=\int_{0}^{1} h(t-\tau)\,d\tau
=\int_{0}^{1} \big[\,t-\tau\,\big]\cdot \mathbf{1}_{\{0<t-\tau<2\}}\,d\tau.

 whenever that interval is nonempty. Evaluate by time ranges:

For : no overlap 

For :


y(t)=\int_{0}^{t}(t-\tau)\,d\tau
=\Big[t\tau-\tfrac{\tau^2}{2}\Big]_{0}^{t}=\frac{t^2}{2}.

For :


y(t)=\int_{0}^{1}(t-\tau)\,d\tau
=\Big[t\tau-\tfrac{\tau^2}{2}\Big]_{0}^{1}=t-\tfrac{1}{2}.

For :


y(t)=\int_{t-2}^{1}(t-\tau)\,d\tau
=\Big[t\tau-\tfrac{\tau^2}{2}\Big]_{t-2}^{1}
=-\tfrac{1}{2}t^2+t+\tfrac{3}{2}.

For : no overlap 


So compactly,

y(t)=\begin{cases}
0, & t\le0,\\[4pt]
\dfrac{t^{2}}{2}, & 0<t<1,\\[6pt]
t-\dfrac{1}{2}, & 1\le t<2,\\[6pt]
-\dfrac{1}{2}t^{2}+t+\dfrac{3}{2}, & 2\le t<3,\\[4pt]
0, & t\ge3.
\end{cases}

(Continuous, piecewise-smooth — looks like a smooth rise, a linear plateau, then a quadratic fall to zero. Clean and predictable — exactly what you want for scaling operations.)

y[n]=\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\min(0,n)} 2^{k}.

So

y[n]=\begin{cases}
2^{\,n+1}, & n<0,\\[4pt]
2, & n\ge0.
\end{cases}

(Discrete result is a geometric tail for negative n that saturates to 2 for nonnegative n. Nice, stable, and summable — like a business with healthy recurring revenue.)

Θ

𝑛
)
.
T(n)=T(n/5)+T(4n/5)+Θ(n).

[04/10/25, 9:05:12 PM] Nishchay(ai): *Hey Geeks!*
Hope you’re all excited to participate in ACM ICPC 2025-26! Below are the steps and important details for the registration process:

All students of SVNIT intending to participate in the ACM International Collegiate Programming Contest (ICPC) 2025-26 must adhere strictly to the following registration protocol. Failure to comply with any step will result in disqualification.

*Steps for Registration:*

ICPC Global Site Registration: All team members must register individually on the ICPC Global portal: https://icpc.global/register. Use your personal Gmail ID for this process.

*Institution Selection*: When prompted for your institution, you must select Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology from the existing dropdown list. Do NOT add a new institution entry.

*Profile Completion*: After logging in, complete all mandatory fields in your personal profile. Incomplete profiles are grounds for team rejection.

*Team Formation*: Form a team of three members. All members must be from SVNIT.

*DO NOT CREATE A TEAM ON THE ICPC DASHBOARD.* Team creation will be handled centrally by the coordinators after your submission is verified. Any self-created teams on the portal will be disregarded.

Fee Payment and Regional Registration:

The registration fee is INR 1100/- per team, per region. A team can register for a maximum of TWO regions.

Google Form Submission: The team leader must fill out the Google Form corresponding to each chosen region. You must use the same Gmail ID that you used for the ICPC Global Site registration.

*AmritapuriRegion Form*: https://forms.gle/MpruMgGMEcuti7kS6

*Kanpur Region Form*: https://forms.gle/5PL5sHskAEcywFHC7

*Chennai Region Form*: https://forms.gle/Lxt81EUDMVfqVQxH8

Confirmation:
Your team's status on the ICPC Global dashboard will change to ACCEPTED after verification, which may take several days. This will serve as your confirmation.

DEADLINE:
The absolute deadline for payment and Google Form submission is *5th October 2025, 11:59 PM IST*. Late entries will not be entertained under any circumstances.

Rules and Regulations:
All participants are required to familiarize themselves with the official contest rules: *https://icpc.global/regionals/rules*

Contact Information:

For Payment-Related Queries ONLY:
*Dhruv Patel: +91 90238 72884*

For All Other Procedural Queries:
*Miten Gandhi: +91 87993 77093*
*Vanshik Godeshwar: +91 74878 69733*

Compliance with this process is mandatory. All the best.

[24/12/25, 11:53:06 AM] Nishchay(ai): Dear Students,

The choice filling for B.Tech. II Year Minor and Honors courses is now open on the MiS portal. Students who wish to register for Minor or Honors courses are kindly requested to submit their willingness on the MiS portal on or before 28th December 2025. Before giving your choice, I request you to please take enough time and understand the course details clearly. Kindly go through the syllabus, and discuss with your teachers and senior students before taking a final decision. Please note that once the course is allotted, change will not be possible later.

Students who are already studying Minor or Honors courses will be automatically offered the next subject after sucessfully completing the academic requirements.

I request you to please inform your friends about this message. Class Representatives are kindly requested to circulate this information in your class groups.

Wishing you all the very best in making the right academic choice.

With best wishes,


Dr. Vimal Patel

Associate Dean (Acad) & I/c. Dy. Reg. (Acad)
